Terminal_Metabolite.reason: When coexpressed with CoTXSS and CoCYP716A392, CoACT1 produced more faradiol palmitate than the control.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: Results state that only CoACT1 and CoACT2 produced more faradiol palmitate than the control when coexpressed with CoTXSS and CoCYP716A392.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: Candidate CoACT genes were predicted to be members of the membrane-bound O-acyltransferase (MBOAT) superfamily.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: CoACT1-3 were more highly expressed in flowers than leaves; CoACT1 and CoACT2 were upregulated in ray florets compared to disk florets; qRT-PCR showed CoACT1 highest in young buds.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: Triterpene fatty acid esters were found exclusively in floral tissue, with ray florets containing much higher amounts.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: Functional testing used transient agroinfiltration/co-expression in N. benthamiana leaves.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: The gene was validated by co-expression with CoTXSS and CoCYP716A392 in N. benthamiana followed by GC-MS analysis.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: All three statements match the Results: CoACT1 is a candidate acyltransferase, increased faradiol palmitate with CoTXSS+CoCYP716A392, and is flower-enriched.

Other_Important_Info.reason: The paper states CoACT1 and CoACT2 are likely homeologues and that all CoACTs except CoACT1 and CoACT6 increased taraxasterol/Ψ-taraxasterol palmitate with CoTXSS alone.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: The study reconstructs faradiol FAE biosynthesis in N. benthamiana using CoACT1 among the required genes, supporting engineering utility.

Omics_Data.reason: Genome accession GCA_964273985.1 and Zenodo 10.5281/zenodo.13869958 are explicitly provided in Data availability.

Applied_Promoters.reason: Constructs were assembled with a CaMV35S promoter.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: Faradiol esters/faradiol FAEs are presented as major contributors to anti-inflammatory floral extract activity.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: CoACT1 was functionally assayed together with CoTXSS and CoCYP716A392, but direct physical protein-protein interaction is not shown.

Source_Species.reason: CoACT2 is a candidate gene from Calendula officinalis.

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The source species is explicitly Calendula officinalis.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: Faradiol palmitate is one of the triterpene fatty acid esters discussed throughout the paper.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: Results explicitly state CoACT2 promoted faradiol palmitate accumulation above control with upstream genes.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: CoACT candidates are predicted MBOAT superfamily members.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: CoACT1-3 were more highly expressed in flowers than leaves; CoACT2 was upregulated in ray florets compared to disk florets; qRT-PCR showed CoACT2 highest in young buds.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: TFAEs accumulate in floral tissue, especially ray florets.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: Gene function was tested by transient agroinfiltration in N. benthamiana.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: Candidate gene co-expression with CoTXSS and CoCYP716A392 in N. benthamiana followed by GC-MS is exactly what was done.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: Each clause is supported: CoACT2 increased faradiol palmitate with CoTXSS+CoCYP716A392, is flower-expressed, and is likely homeologous with CoACT1.

Other_Important_Info.reason: The paper explicitly states CoACT1 and CoACT2 are likely homeologues encoded in syntenic regions.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: CoACT2 is included among genes identified for production of faradiol FAEs, supporting pathway reconstitution use.

Omics_Data.reason: The listed genome and Zenodo resources are in the Data availability section.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: Faradiol esters are linked to anti-inflammatory activity of pot marigold floral extracts.

Source_Species.reason: CoACT3 is one of the pot marigold candidate ACT genes.

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The source species is Calendula officinalis.

Applied_Species.reason: CoACT3 was characterized in Nicotiana benthamiana transient expression assays.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The host used was Nicotiana benthamiana.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: With CoTXSS alone, CoACT3 produced the largest quantities of Ψ-taraxasterol/taraxasterol palmitate.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: These palmitate products are triterpene fatty acid esters.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: The Results explicitly say CoACT3 produced the largest quantities of Ψ-taraxasterol/taraxasterol palmitate among tested CoACTs.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: Candidate CoACT genes are predicted members of the MBOAT superfamily.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: The paper states CoACT1-3 were all more highly expressed in flowers than leaves.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: Triterpene fatty acid esters were reported exclusively in floral tissues.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: Function was tested by transient agroinfiltration in N. benthamiana.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: Co-expression with CoTXSS in N. benthamiana followed by GC-MS is directly described.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: The three summary points align with the Results: CoACT3 acts on taraxasterol/Ψ-taraxasterol scaffolds, gave the highest scaffold palmitate, and was not identified as the faradiol-palmitate ACT.

Other_Important_Info.reason: The paper shows CoACT3 is active with CoTXSS alone and only CoACT1/2 increased faradiol palmitate with CoTXSS+CoCYP716A392.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: Its utility for engineering scaffold ester diversity is a reasonable inference, but this application value is not directly demonstrated in the paper.

Omics_Data.reason: The cited genome accession and Zenodo resource are present in Data availability.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: The paper does not establish strong bioactivity for taraxasterol/Ψ-taraxasterol palmitate; the wording reflects that bioactivity was not established as strongly as for faradiol esters.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: CoTXSS was coexpressed with CoACT3 for pathway testing, but direct interaction was not assessed.

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The source species is explicitly Calendula officinalis.

Applied_Species.reason: CoTXSS was characterized by transient expression in Nicotiana benthamiana.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The host species was Nicotiana benthamiana.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: CoTXSS is connected in the pathway to faradiol and faradiol palmitate; faradiol myristate is a native plant metabolite in the pathway though reconstructed production emphasized palmitate rather than myristate.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: The paper elucidates pentacyclic triterpenoid/faradiol fatty acid ester biosynthesis.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: CoTXSS is the taraxasterol/Ψ-taraxasterol branch synthase.

Enzyme_Name.reason: The paper identifies CoTXSS as a taraxasterol/Ψ-taraxasterol synthase.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: CoTXSS is an oxidosqualene cyclase/squalene cyclase family member.

EC_Number.reason: The specific EC number 5.4.99.- does not appear in the paper.

Primary_Substrate.reason: OSCs synthesize triterpene scaffolds from linear 2,3-oxidosqualene; this is explicitly stated.

Primary_Product.reason: GC-MS showed primary products were taraxasterol or Ψ-taraxasterol, with trace β-amyrin and lupeol.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: This accurately summarizes the described cyclization reaction from 2,3-oxidosqualene to the pentacyclic scaffolds.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: The OSC catalyzing this step is described as the first committed step.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: CoTXSS forms the scaffold precursor for faradiol and its esters.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: It is clearly the first committed and branch-defining step, but 'rate-limiting' is not demonstrated.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: CoTXSS enables formation of Ψ-taraxasterol-derived pathway products and determines scaffold specificity.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: One candidate CoTXSS was predominantly floral; qRT-PCR showed CoTXSS highest in young buds; the other candidate was not expressed and another copy contained mutations indicative of pseudogenization.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: Ψ-taraxasterol and derivatives/faradiol FAEs accumulate in floral tissue, especially ray florets.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: Transient agroinfiltration in N. benthamiana with tHMGR and P19 is explicitly described.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: Genome/transcriptome mining, phylogenetics, transient expression, GC-MS, mutagenesis, and structural modelling were all used.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: All three points are consistent with Results and figures 3–5.

Other_Important_Info.reason: The paper supports more Ψ-taraxasterol than taraxasterol for CoTXSS and identifies I367/E371 and G380/D385/H492/P751 as residues affecting specificity.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: CoTXSS is central to pathway reconstruction in heterologous hosts.

Omics_Data.reason: Genome accession GCA_964273985.1, read accession PRJEB80524, and Zenodo data are explicitly listed.

Applied_Promoters.reason: Binary constructs used the CaMV35S promoter.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: Faradiol and its esters are shown to be anti-inflammatory; faradiol suppresses IL-6 release and reduces STAT3 phosphorylation.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: The enzyme was multifunctional and produced trace β-amyrin and lupeol as side-products.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: These proteins were coexpressed in pathway reconstruction, but the paper does not demonstrate direct physical interactions between CoTXSS and these partners; tHMGR and P19 are co-expression components rather than interacting proteins.

corrections.reason: The specific EC number 5.4.99.- does not appear in the paper.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: The paper characterizes this gene in pentacyclic triterpenoid/faradiol biosynthesis.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: It acts in C16 hydroxylation of the Ψ-taraxasterol branch.

Enzyme_Name.reason: The gene is named CoCYP716A392 in the Results.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: It is a cytochrome P450 in the CYP716A clade/subfamily.

EC_Number.reason: The specific EC number 1.14.-.- is not provided in the paper.

Primary_Substrate.reason: Results indicate the enzyme uses Ψ-taraxasterol, β-amyrin and lupeol as substrates.

Primary_Product.reason: Co-expression yielded faradiol together with smaller quantities of maniladiol and calenduladiol.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: The paper explicitly states CoCYP716A392 is a C16β-hydroxylase able to use Ψ-taraxasterol, β-amyrin and lupeol as substrates.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: This is a downstream decorating/hydroxylation step after scaffold formation.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: It directly forms faradiol, a bioactive intermediate and precursor to faradiol esters.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: It is clearly an important downstream tailoring step, but 'rate-limiting' is not tested.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: The enzyme promotes formation of faradiol, the anti-inflammatory C16-hydroxylated product.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: Differential expression showed CoCYP716A392 predominantly in floral tissues; qRT-PCR showed highest expression in mature buds.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: Faradiol esters and related pathway products accumulate in floral tissue, especially ray florets.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: The paper notes transient agroinfiltration in N. benthamiana and endogenous metabolism may derivatize products.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: Phylogenetics, floral expression, co-expression in N. benthamiana, GC-MS, mutagenesis, and structural modelling were used.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: All three points are supported by the Results and pathway reconstruction section.

Other_Important_Info.reason: CoCYP716A392 is described as likely homeologous with CoCYP716A393, and A285V/A357L/H424R mutagenesis effects are reported for CoCYP716A392.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: It is one of the identified genes enabling reconstruction of faradiol/faradiol palmitate production in N. benthamiana.

Omics_Data.reason: The specified genome accession, PRJEB80524 reads, and Zenodo resource are present in the paper.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: Faradiol strongly inhibited LPS-induced IL-6 release and reduced STAT3 phosphorylation.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: The paper discusses substrate promiscuity and endogenous N. benthamiana metabolism confounding product recovery.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: CoCYP716A392 was functionally combined with CoTXSS and CoACT1/2, but direct physical interactions are not shown.
